title: Extended real number line
content:
In mathematics, the affinely extended real number system is obtained from the real number system formula_1 by adding two infinity elements: formula_2 and where the infinities are treated as actual numbers. It is useful in describing the algebra on infinities and the various limiting behavior s in calculus and mathematical analysis, especially in the theory of measure and integration. The affinely extended real number system is denoted formula_3 or formula_4 or It is the Dedekind–MacNeille completion of the real numbers. When the meaning is clear from context, the symbol formula_2 is often written simply as Motivation.

Limits. It is often useful to describe the behavior of a function as either the argument formula_6 or the function value formula_7 gets "infinitely large" in some sense. For example, consider the function The graph of this function has a horizontal asymptote at Geometrically, when moving increasingly farther to the right along the formula_6-axis, the value of formula_10 approaches 0. This limiting behavior is similar to the limit of a function formula_11 in which the real number formula_6 approaches except that there is no real number to which formula_6 approaches.

By adjoining the elements formula_2 and formula_15 to it enables a formulation of a "limit at infinity", with topological properties similar to those for To make things completely formal, the Cauchy sequences definition of formula_1 allows defining formula_2 as the set of all sequences formula_18 of rational numbers, such that every formula_19 is associated with a corresponding formula_20 for which formula_21 for all The definition of formula_15 can be constructed similarly. Measure and integration. In measure theory, it is often useful to allow sets that have infinite measure and integrals whose value may be infinite.

Such measures arise naturally out of calculus. For example, in assigning a measure to formula_1 that agrees with the usual length of intervals, this measure must be larger than any finite real number. Also, when considering improper integral s, such as the value "infinity" arises. Finally, it is often useful to consider the limit of a sequence of functions, such as Without allowing functions to take on infinite values, such essential results as the monotone convergence theorem and the dominated convergence theorem would not make sense. Order and topological properties.

The affinely extended real number system can be turned into a totally ordered set, by defining formula_26 for all With this order topology, formula_3 has the desirable property of compactness: every subset of formula_28 has a supremum and an infimum (the infimum of the empty set is formula_29 and its supremum is formula_15). Moreover, with this topology, formula_28 is homeomorphic to the unit interval Thus the topology is metrizable, corresponding (for a given homeomorphism) to the ordinary metric on this interval.

There is no metric that is an extension of the ordinary metric on In this topology, a set formula_32 is a neighborhood of if and only if it contains a set formula_33 for some real number The notion of the neighborhood of formula_15 can be defined similarly. Using this characterization of extended-real neighborhoods, the specially defined limits for formula_6 tending to formula_2 and and the specially defined concepts of limits equal to formula_2 and reduce to the general topological definition of limits. Arithmetic operations.

The arithmetic operations of formula_1 can be partially extended to formula_28 as follows: For exponentiation, see. Here, means both and while means both and The expressions formula_41 and formula_42 (called indeterminate form s) are usually left undefined. These rules are modeled on the laws for infinite limits.
